original_abstract:
Bacterial wound infections pose a significant global healthcare challenge due to the rapid rise of antibiotic-resistant pathogens. In response, this study introduces an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy utilizing a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme designed for accelerated wound healing. The composite (Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs, or FMA NPs) was synthesized by integrating ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) into an IRMOF-3 shell that encapsulates superparamagnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) cores. 

The FMA nanozymes demonstrate synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity, effectively catalyzing low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) to produce highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In vitro experiments confirmed that the hybrid nanozymes possess excellent biocompatibility and potent bactericidal efficacy against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, including the successful disruption of established biofilms. In vivo assessments using a mouse model of infected wounds further revealed that FMA NPs significantly promote tissue repair and re-epithelialization with high biosafety and no systemic toxicity. These results highlight the potential of FMA hybrid nanozymes as a robust and biocompatible platform for the clinical management of bacterial infections and wound care without the need for traditional antibiotics.
humanized_abstract:
Bacterial wound infections constitute a major global health challenge due to increasing levels of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Here, we report an antimicrobial strategy towards rapid wound healing without the need for antibiotics, based on a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme. A novel iron oxide cores ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) embedded into an IRMOF-3 shell as the Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs (FMA NPs) composite were constructed for the first time.

The FMA nanozymes had a synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD) activity, and could catalyze low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) into highly reactive hydroxyl radical (·OH). In vitro, hybrid nanozymes showed excellent biocompatibility while demonstrated strong bactericidal activities towards both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) by destroying biofilms. In a mouse wound infected model, the FMA NPs promoted rapid wound healing with high biosafety and no systematic toxicity. Overall, our findings demonstrated that FMA hybrid nanozymes had great potential in treating antibiotic-resistant bacterial infection and improving wound healing therapy in a clinical application without using any antibiotics.

The FMA nanozymes had a synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD) activity, and could catalyze low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) into highly reactive hydroxyl radical (·OH). In vitro, hybrid nanozymes showed excellent biocompatibility while demonstrated strong bactericidal activities towards both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) by destroying biofilms. In a mouse wound infected model, the FMA NPs promoted rapid wound healing with high biosafety and no systematic toxicity. Overall, our findings demonstrated that FMA hybrid nanozymes had great potential in treating antibiotic-resistant bacterial infection and improving wound healing therapy in a clinical application without using any antibiotics.
document.input:
Bacterial wound infections pose a significant global healthcare challenge due to the rapid rise of antibiotic-resistant pathogens. In response, this study introduces an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy utilizing a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme designed for accelerated wound healing. The composite (Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs, or FMA NPs) was synthesized by integrating ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) into an IRMOF-3 shell that encapsulates superparamagnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) cores. 

The FMA nanozymes demonstrate synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity, effectively catalyzing low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) to produce highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In vivo assessments using a mouse model of infected wounds further revealed that FMA NPs significantly promote tissue repair and re-epithelialization with high biosafety and no systemic toxicity. These results highlight the potential of FMA hybrid nanozymes as a robust and biocompatible platform for the clinical management of bacterial infections and wound care without the need for traditional antibiotics.
